values.keywords: primary color, primary palette, primary
values.answer: Three primary colors: Black (#000000) is the foundation, HCFM Blue (#0047BB / Pantone 2728C) is the identifier, Yellow Gold (#FFB500 / Pantone 7549C) is the energy.

values.keywords: secondary color, accent, accent palette
values.answer: Three secondary colors: Marian Blue (#00A9E0 / 2995C) pairs with Playlist Script. Muted Gold (#89764B / 871C) is for print and donor materials. White is for text on dark backgrounds.

values.keywords: liturgical, lent, advent, pentecost, ordinary time, extended
values.answer: Extended palette: Purple (#5F259F) for Lent, Advent, and penitential themes. Red (#CF3339) for Pentecost, martyrs, Sacred Heart. Green (#3A913F) for Ordinary Time, growth, creation care.

values.keywords: hcfm blue, pantone 2728, brand blue, identifier blue
values.answer: HCFM Blue. Hex #0047BB · RGB 0/71/187 · Pantone 2728C. The brand identifier — the logo, the mark, formal letterhead, official documents, website headers. Use sparingly outside those moments so it stays meaningful.

values.keywords: yellow gold, pantone 7549, gold hex, ffb500
values.answer: Yellow Gold. Hex #FFB500 · RGB 255/181/0 · Pantone 7549C. The energy color. Headlines on dark backgrounds, call-to-action buttons, accent borders, Playlist Script, icons that need to pop. Never for body text on white.

values.keywords: marian blue, pantone 2995, sky blue, light blue
values.answer: Marian Blue. Hex #00A9E0 · RGB 0/169/224 · Pantone 2995C. Pairs with Playlist Script. Closely associated with Mary — gentleness, presence, guidance. The brand book sometimes calls this "Light Blue" — same color.

values.keywords: muted gold, pantone 871, old gold, 89764b
values.answer: Muted Gold. Hex #89764B · RGB 137/118/75 · Pantone 871C. The reverent gold. Print, formal, donor materials, certificates. Symbolizes sacredness in traditional, devotional contexts.

values.keywords: black, foundation color, 000000
values.answer: Black (#000000). Process Black. Your workhorse. The primary background that lets every other brand color shine. Use it for social graphics, video thumbnails, and as a 40 to 70 percent overlay on photos.

values.keywords: white, ffffff, breathing space
values.answer: White (#FFFFFF). Visual rest and balance. The space between things. Use white as text on dark, and as negative space to let content breathe.

values.keywords: yellow gold vs muted gold, when use muted gold, two golds, gold difference
values.answer: Simple rule: If it is on a screen, use Yellow Gold. If it is for donors, on a certificate, or in formal print, use Muted Gold. Yellow Gold is digital and energetic. Muted Gold is traditional and reverent. The two golds are not interchangeable.

values.keywords: yellow gold body text, gold on white, accessibility, contrast
values.answer: Never use Yellow Gold for body text on white. Yellow Gold on white is 1.9:1 contrast — fails WCAG AA. Use Yellow Gold for headlines and accents only on dark backgrounds.

values.keywords: contrast, wcag, readable, accessibility ratio
values.answer: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for body text, 3:1 for large text. HCFM Blue on White: 8.6:1 (AAA). White on HCFM Blue: 8.6:1 (AAA). Yellow Gold on Black: 11.7:1 (AAA). Yellow Gold on White: 1.9:1 (FAILS).

values.keywords: light blue, why marian
values.answer: "Light Blue" in the 2026 brand book and "Marian Blue" in the Visual Identity deck refer to the same color (#00A9E0). Going forward, we standardize on "Marian Blue" because it carries the Marian meaning explicitly.

values.keywords: extended palette, do not use, old colors
values.answer: The old SharePoint extended palette (red, orange, dark green, magenta, light pink, sage, etc. — 14 colors total) is deprecated. The 2026 system is six colors only: 4 primary, 2 accent, plus three liturgical (Purple, Red, Green) for seasonal use.

values.keywords: mark vs logo, mark or logo, difference mark logo, symbol vs logo
values.answer: The Mark is the symbol alone. Use when space is small or the audience already knows HCFM (favicons, profile pictures, watermarks). The Logo is the symbol with "Holy Cross Family Ministries." Use when introducing HCFM, or for official documents.

values.keywords: logotype 1, horizontal, wide layout
values.answer: Logotype 1 — Horizontal. Mark left, wordmark right on a single horizontal line. Best for wide layouts: web headers, document headers, email signatures.

values.keywords: logotype 2, stacked
values.answer: Logotype 2 — Stacked. Mark on top, wordmark on two lines below. The default formal version. Use on letterhead, certificates, official documents.

values.keywords: logotype 3, compact stacked
values.answer: Logotype 3 — Compact stacked. Mark on top with a smaller wordmark. For tight spaces where the stacked version is too tall but you still need full identification.

values.keywords: logotype 4, single line, narrow
values.answer: Logotype 4 — Single line. Mark with the full wordmark on a single line. Use in narrow horizontal contexts: footers, banners, sign-off blocks.

values.keywords: logo download, where logo, download logo, get logo
values.answer: Logo files are gated on the Downloads tab. Public packs include PNG and JPG only. Editable AI source files are restricted to brand owners (Victoria and Emmanuel). Email vhassan@hcfm.org or eepau@hcfm.org for access.

values.keywords: ai file, editable, illustrator, source file, eps
values.answer: AI / EPS / editable source files are restricted to the brand owners (Victoria and Emmanuel). This is intentional: a single canonical source keeps every ministry-center logo identical. If you need a custom edit, request it through the Help tab.

values.keywords: minimum size, logo size, how small
values.answer: Mark alone: 5/16 inch (7.94 mm) print, 150 px web. Wordmark "FAMILY MINISTRIES" cap height: 1/16 inch (1.59 mm). Below these sizes, legibility breaks.

values.keywords: clear space, free space, margin around logo
values.answer: Clear space around the mark equals the height of one prayer-person bead in the mark itself. Keep that minimum margin on all four sides — never let other elements crowd inside it.

values.keywords: logo do not, misuse, never do, logo rule
values.answer: Never: stretch or distort the logo · use colors outside the approved palette · substitute fonts on the wordmark · add drop shadows, glows, or 3D · add strokes or outlines · crop in a way that obscures the form · rotate the mark · place on busy backgrounds without an overlay.

values.keywords: file format, svg, png, jpg
values.answer: Public logo packs include PNG and JPG only. SVG, EPS, and AI are brand-owner restricted. If you need a specific format we have not packaged (vector outlines, white-only PDF, etc.), request through the Help page.

values.keywords: favicon, profile picture, small
values.answer: For favicons and social profile pictures, use the Mark alone (just the symbol, no wordmark). Render at 800px+ and export down to platform sizes. Approved colors: HCFM Blue or Yellow Gold on white, or White on HCFM Blue.

values.keywords: symbol meaning, what does symbol, meaning of mark, mark layers
values.answer:
The HCFM symbol carries five layers of meaning:
1. 10 Beads of the Rosary — totality and unity.
2. A Family of Prayer — each shape is a person in prayer.
3. Floral Character — Rose, Lily, Iris (symbolic of Mary).
4. Moon and Light — center white space emits light.
5. Welcome and Invitation — radiates outward.

values.keywords: 10 beads, ten beads, rosary beads, why ten
values.answer: The mark is composed of 10 interconnected shapes — a reference to the 10 beads of a Rosary decade. The design represents totality and unity: family members of every kind brought together in prayer, and the continuous rhythm of prayer lived daily, not occasionally.

values.keywords: family of prayer, person, figure
values.answer: Each shape in the mark represents a person in prayer — hands raised or folded. The ten shapes together form a united family. No single shape stands alone; each depends on the others. Family prayer strengthens relationships and faith.

values.keywords: floral, rose, lily, iris, mary connection
values.answer: The mark expresses a floral character — Rose, Lily, Iris, all flowers symbolic of the Virgin Mary. The petal-like shape of each unit is intentional. When you let your eye soften, the mark reads less like ten beads and more like a flower in full bloom.

values.keywords: moon, light center, white space mark
values.answer: The white space at the heart of the mark creates an optical effect that seems to emit light — a reference to the moon. In Catholic iconography, Mary is often shown standing on the moon, clothed in light. The mark carries that imagery without ever drawing it literally.

values.keywords: welcome, invitation, mission, outward
values.answer: The symbol radiates outward by design. HCFM is not inward-facing — we are mission-driven, actively reaching out to families across cultures and communities. The careful delineation of form also suggests the sense of mystery associated with the work of the Holy Spirit.

values.keywords: font, typography, fonts list
values.answer: HCFM uses three fonts. Whitney for headlines (sans-serif, 6 weights). Calluna for body text (serif, 4 weights). Playlist Script for decorative single words (Yellow Gold, White, or Marian Blue only).

values.keywords: body text, body font, paragraph font
values.answer: Use Calluna for body text. A serif font that reflects tradition, depth, and reflection. Calluna is for paragraphs, prayer text, reflections, and formal documents. Available weights: Regular, Italic, Semibold, Bold.

values.keywords: headline, header, display font
values.answer: Use Whitney for headlines, subheadings, and short text. Six weights are available: Light, Book, Medium, Semibold, Bold, Black. Whitney is clean, modern, and highly readable.

values.keywords: playlist script, script font, decorative font
values.answer: Playlist Script rules: Single words or short phrases only (3–4 words max). One element per design. Yellow Gold, White, or Marian Blue only. Never at small sizes (under 18px screen / 14pt print). Never for body text.

values.keywords: whitney, sans serif
values.answer: Whitney is HCFM's display sans-serif. Designed by Hoefler & Frere-Jones. Six weights: Light, Book, Medium, Semibold, Bold, Black. Use for headlines, navigation, buttons, callouts, captions, labels — anywhere clarity at a glance matters.

values.keywords: calluna, serif font
values.answer: Calluna is HCFM's body serif. Designed by Jos Buivenga. Four weights: Regular, Italic, Semibold, Bold. Use for paragraphs, prayer text, reflections, letterhead body, donor letters, and anywhere a serif's warmth is appropriate.

values.keywords: times roman, times new roman
values.answer: Avoid Times Roman / Times New Roman. The older SharePoint guidance suggesting Times Roman for letter body is superseded — use Calluna instead. Calluna carries the warmth Times Roman lacks.

values.keywords: font download, where font, get fonts
values.answer: Font files are on the Downloads tab under the Fonts pane. Whitney pack (368 KB), Calluna pack (252 KB), Playlist Script (52 KB), or all three in one HCFM Font Pack (660 KB).

values.keywords: font pairing, combination, mix fonts
values.answer: Default pairing: Whitney + Calluna (headline + body). For hero moments, campaign covers, donor letters, and feast-day communications, add Playlist Script for one decorative word: Whitney + Playlist Script + Calluna.

values.keywords: voice, tone, how to write
values.answer: HCFM has two registers. For the faithful: reverent, warm, family-first, narrative, named-not-abstract. For vendors: specific, direct, practical, documented. The family is the hero, prayer is the language, we are the support.

values.keywords: we say, preferred phrase, always say
values.answer: We say: "The family that prays together stays together." (Father Peyton's actual phrase.) "Holy Cross Family Ministries" spelled out on first reference, "HCFM" after. "Venerable Patrick Peyton, C.S.C." with title and order on first reference. "Father Peyton" on second reference. Country names by name.

values.keywords: we avoid, never say, do not say
values.answer: We avoid: theological jargon where plain English works. "Intercessory familial communion" when we mean family prayer. "Stakeholders" or "audience segments" for the people we serve. Hyperbole — we do not "transform lives." Generic mission language. Acronyms before they are spelled out.

values.keywords: donor letter, how donor letter
values.answer: Donor letters: warm, grateful, named. Open with "Dear friends in Christ," or "Dear [Name]," and reference something specific the donor has supported. Calluna for body. Sign with full name and title.

values.keywords: social caption, caption tone
values.answer: Social captions: plain, devotional, short. "Today, we honor the Holy Name of Jesus…" works better than "Join us as we celebrate…" Aim for 1–2 sentences plus a closing prayer or question.

values.keywords: imagery, photography, photo categories
values.answer: Five sample categories of photography: Father Patrick Peyton, ministry-center culture, Marian imagery, family prayer, Rosary imagery. Use high-quality images only. Apply dark overlays when text goes over a photo. Choose real over staged.

values.keywords: rule of thirds
values.answer: Rule of thirds: divide the image into nine equal parts with two vertical and two horizontal lines. Place the main subject at the intersections or along the lines to create a balanced scene.

values.keywords: leading lines
values.answer: Leading lines: use natural elements (rivers, roads, pathways) or man-made elements (fences, walls, staircases) to create a visual path that guides the viewer's eye to the subject.

values.keywords: frame within frame
values.answer: Frame within a frame: use natural elements like trees or rocks, or man-made objects like windows and doorways, to draw attention to the subject. Adds depth and visual interest.

values.keywords: filling the frame
values.answer: Filling the frame: move closer to the subject. The focus falls on facial expression and details that would otherwise be missed. Bonus: distracting backgrounds disappear.

values.keywords: shot list, event photography
values.answer: For HCFM events: shoot details (signs, programs, branded items), venue (before and during), attendees (candid emotional shots — both close-up and wide), speakers and special guests. A shot list separates a recap from a memory.

values.keywords: stock photography, staged photo
values.answer: Avoid stock photography. Choose real over staged: real families, real prayer, real local context. The image must reflect HCFM's actual mission, not a generic "international" aesthetic.

values.keywords: design element, border, fade, curve
values.answer: Four approved design elements: Thin Border (3-4 px max), Color Fade Effect (brand colors only), Curved Shapes, Dark Overlays. Use only one or two per design.

values.keywords: thin border
values.answer: Thin Border: a 3–4 pixel frame in a brand color. Use Muted Gold for traditional, reverent contexts. Use Yellow Gold for digital and energetic applications. Where it works: certificate edges, donor cards, prayer-card edges, formal invitations.

values.keywords: color fade, gradient
values.answer: Color Fade Effect: a gradient using only brand-palette colors. Yellow Gold to Black is the digital default. HCFM Blue to White works for formal moments. Never introduce outside colors into a brand fade.

values.keywords: dark overlay, photo overlay opacity
values.answer: Dark Overlays: black transparent layer on photos to make text readable. Adjust opacity by eye — light images with sparse text need 40 to 50 percent, busy images with longer copy need 60 to 70 percent. The rule is readability, not a number.

values.keywords: 60-30-10, sixty thirty ten, color ratio
values.answer: The 60-30-10 rule: 60% dominant color (background), 30% secondary (supporting), 10% accent (highlights). Sometimes 70-20-10 or 80-10-10 is better. The point is intentional hierarchy: one color leading, the others playing their part.

values.keywords: stationery, letterhead, business card, envelope
values.answer: Print specs: Letterhead 8.5×11 in on Strathmore 24# Bright White Wove with Pantone 871U + 2728U inks. Business card 3.5×2 in on Strathmore 130# DTC Cover. #10 envelope 9.5×4.125 in on the same letterhead stock. Mailing label 5.625×4 in on Crack-n-Peel.

values.keywords: letterhead template, word template
values.answer: Editable Word letterhead templates exist for the parent ministry and six sub-ministries (Catholic Mom, Family Rosary, Family Theater Productions, Father Peyton Prayer Guild, Museum of Family Prayer, plus the parent HCFM letterhead). Available on the Downloads tab.

values.keywords: easton address, mailing address
values.answer: HCFM Easton headquarters: 508 Washington Street, North Easton, MA 02356, USA.

values.keywords: instagram size, instagram post, instagram dimensions
values.answer: Instagram: static portrait 1080×1350 px · Reel/Story 1080×1920 px. Use exactly these dimensions — designs that fall short get cropped, designs that overshoot get downsized awkwardly.

values.keywords: facebook size, facebook dimensions
values.answer: Facebook: static portrait 1080×1350 px · video (feed) 1280×720 px · Reels 1080×1920 px.

values.keywords: linkedin size, linkedin dimensions
values.answer: LinkedIn: static image 1200×627 px · portrait image 1080×1350 px.

values.keywords: twitter, x size, x dimensions
values.answer: X (Twitter): static image 1200×675 px.

values.keywords: youtube thumbnail, youtube size
values.answer: YouTube: thumbnail 1280×720 px (16:9 ratio).

values.keywords: ministry center, sub ministry, ministries list
values.answer: HCFM has 24 ministry centers across 18 countries on 6 continents. The Ministry Centers tab lists every one with its logo gallery. Each click opens that ministry's logo pack.

values.keywords: family rosary
values.answer: Family Rosary is the founding ministry of HCFM, based in Easton, MA. Father Peyton's original Rosary Crusade has grown into a global ministry through Family Rosary Crusade chapters in the Philippines, Latin America, Ireland, and beyond.

values.keywords: father peyton, venerable patrick peyton, founder
values.answer: Venerable Patrick Peyton, C.S.C. (1909–1992) founded Holy Cross Family Ministries. His phrase "The family that prays together stays together" anchors HCFM's mission. "Father Peyton" on second reference, never just "Patrick."

values.keywords: catholic mom
values.answer: Catholic Mom is one of HCFM's digital ministries based in Easton, MA. Logo pack is available in the Ministry Centers directory.

values.keywords: museum of family prayer
values.answer: The Museum of Family Prayer in Easton, MA preserves and celebrates the heritage of Father Peyton's mission and the broader history of Catholic family prayer.

values.keywords: family theater productions
values.answer: Family Theater Productions, based in Hollywood, CA, is HCFM's media arm. Founded by Father Peyton and led today by Father David Guffey, C.S.C.

values.keywords: preflight, pre-flight, checklist, before publish
values.answer: Run through the Pre-flight Checklist before you publish. Four sections: Colors, Fonts, Design Elements, Platform. Most common ship-blockers: Yellow Gold body text on white (fails contrast), too many design elements (clutter), wrong platform dimensions (cropping).

values.keywords: review my work, who reviews, check my design
values.answer: Email vhassan@hcfm.org or eepau@hcfm.org before you ship anything you are unsure about. No charge, no formal request process. Turnaround is typically two business days.

values.keywords: co-branding, partner logo, two logos
values.answer: Email Victoria or Emmanuel before you design anything with a partner organization's logo. We have specific rules about how the two logos sit together, the spacing between them, and what needs approval first.

values.keywords: report misuse, wrong use
values.answer: Email Victoria or Emmanuel with a screenshot or link. We follow up directly with whoever is using the brand wrongly. If it is someone outside HCFM (a vendor, a third-party site), we handle the legal follow-up.

values.keywords: contact, email, help
values.answer: Email vhassan@hcfm.org (Victoria Hassan) or eepau@hcfm.org (Emmanuel Epau). Either inbox is fine — both forms on the Help page go to both of us.

values.keywords: password, access, unlock
values.answer: Brand asset downloads are gated. Email Victoria or Emmanuel to request the access password. Source files (AI) are restricted to brand owners.

values.keywords: transition, deadline, 2026 system
values.answer: All ministry centers are expected to transition to the 2026 system. No hard deadline. New materials use the new system. Existing materials phase out over 6–12 months.

values.keywords: translation, french, spanish, portuguese, swahili
values.answer: French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Swahili translations are in progress. Until they are published, ministry centers should use the English version and ask Easton for clarification.

values.keywords: who maintains, who owns brand, easton creatives, who manages brand
values.answer: The HCFM brand is maintained by Victoria Hassan and Emmanuel Epau, working out of the HCFM headquarters in North Easton, MA. Any update to the brand book or this brand portal flows through them. They are sometimes called the "Easton Creatives" team in internal emails — same people.

values.keywords: why changed, why update, why new system
values.answer: The 2026 system simplified the palette from 20 colors to 6, elevated Yellow Gold to primary (over Muted Gold) for digital, and added Playlist Script as a decorative third typeface. The changes responded to ministry-center feedback and produced measurably stronger engagement on social platforms.

values.keywords: complementary colors, opposite colors, why blue and yellow work, color wheel
values.answer: Blue and Yellow Gold are complementary colors — they sit on opposite sides of the color wheel. The brain processes complementary pairs faster than any other combination because each color makes the other appear more vibrant. That neurological speed is exactly what you want when you have two seconds to stop a scroll.

values.keywords: why blue yellow, ikea best buy walmart, industry blue yellow
values.answer: Blue + Yellow appears in IKEA, Best Buy, and Walmart for a reason: Blue signals trust, reliability, stability. Yellow signals energy, warmth, optimism. Together they communicate "dependable and exciting at the same time." That exact signal is also what a faith-based ministry needs — trustworthy enough to support, alive enough to engage.

values.keywords: gestalt, figure ground, how brain processes color
values.answer: Figure-ground perception (a Gestalt principle) is how the brain separates foreground from background. The higher the contrast, the faster the separation. On social media — fractions of a second to decide — high contrast lets the brain do its job in the smallest possible time. A dark background with high-contrast Yellow Gold is the easiest job a scrolling brain can be given.

values.keywords: why yellow on black, gold on black, high contrast
values.answer: Yellow Gold on Black gives a contrast ratio of 11.7:1 — passes WCAG AAA standards. The same Yellow Gold on HCFM Blue is only 4.5:1 — passes AA but is roughly 2.5x less visible. Black gives Yellow Gold maximum punch. That punch matters in a feed.

values.keywords: why dark, why black background, dark mode
values.answer: More than 80% of users browse with dark mode on. Black backgrounds are not just an aesthetic choice — they are how most families actually experience content on their phones. Designing for dark backgrounds means designing for how people are already consuming content. There is also an accessibility win: every HCFM brand color (Yellow Gold, White, Marian Blue) passes AAA contrast on Black.

values.keywords: attention vs affinity, stop scroll vs trust, engagement quality
values.answer: There are two kinds of color performance. Attention metrics (does it stop the scroll?) and affinity metrics (does it earn trust and a share?). High-contrast colors stop the scroll. But a color that grabs attention without inspiring trust hurts mission engagement. The HCFM system threads the needle: dark backgrounds for scroll-stop, Marian Blue and warm Calluna prose for the trust that follows.

values.keywords: why 60-30-10, color hierarchy why, why one color leads
values.answer: 60-30-10 works because when every color fights for equal attention, the eye doesn't know where to land. The whole composition feels busy and you can't put your finger on why. Letting one color dominate (60%), one support (30%), and one accent (10%) gives the eye a clear path. Sometimes 70-20-10 or 80-10-10 is better — the principle is intentional hierarchy, not exact percentages.

values.keywords: where does 60-30-10 come from, origin of rule
values.answer: The 60-30-10 rule is a classic principle that shows up across design fields — interior decorating, fashion, graphic design. It is not an HCFM invention. We adopted it because it makes color decisions reproducible: any HCFM creative can audit a design by asking "is one color clearly leading?" If the answer is yes and the leader is a brand color, the design is on track.

values.keywords: why yellow gold primary, why elevated yellow gold, gold change 2026
values.answer: In 2026 we elevated Yellow Gold (#FFB500 / Pantone 7549C) to primary for digital. Why: Muted Gold (#89764B) is reverent and traditional but lacks scroll-stopping power on small screens. Yellow Gold has the brightness and saturation digital feeds reward. Muted Gold remains the right choice for print, donor materials, and certificates — the two golds serve different registers.

values.keywords: muted gold problem, why not muted gold digital
values.answer: Muted Gold (#89764B) is beautiful in print, but on social feeds it reads as olive or brown — too muted to register at a glance. Plus its contrast on white is only 3.4:1 (fails WCAG AA for body text). Yellow Gold solves both problems while keeping the gold metaphor that anchors HCFM's brand to liturgical glory.

values.keywords: why blue mary, mary blue tradition, why marian blue
values.answer: The association of Blue with the Virgin Mary dates to the 12th century, when blue pigment (lapis lazuli) was the most expensive available — reserved for the most sacred subjects. The convention spread across Europe and remains universal in Catholic iconography. Marian Blue (#00A9E0) carries that history without needing to draw it.

values.keywords: why blue support not lead, blue 10 percent digital, blue accent
values.answer: In digital, Blue is calm — it signals trust and reliability. But calm doesn't stop a scroll. Yellow Gold on Black grabs first. Once attention is captured, Blue keeps the viewer engaged. That's why Blue plays a 10% accent role digitally — links, secondary buttons, dividers — and a much larger role in print where attention is already given. The roles flip by medium.

values.keywords: why hcfm blue 0047bb, pantone 2728 reason
values.answer: HCFM Blue (#0047BB / Pantone 2728C) was selected because it is a saturated, confident blue — strong enough to anchor formal materials but not so dark it loses warmth. It tests well across cultures, has no negative national associations, and pairs naturally with both Yellow Gold (energetic) and White (formal).

values.keywords: black culture mourning, east africa black, death color
values.answer: In East Africa, black clothing outside formal settings can trigger bereavement associations. Black-bordered or desaturated images are a recognized social-media death-announcement convention. We design knowing this: dark backgrounds with bright HCFM colors on top read as energetic, not somber. The presence of Yellow Gold and White prevents the misread.

values.keywords: philippines black, asia color culture
values.answer: In the Philippines, Black carries traditional mourning weight but is moderated by Western media influence. Urban audiences read black as fashion or elegance. Older and rural audiences keep stronger funeral associations. The dark-with-Yellow-Gold combination reads as modern and confident, not mournful — but ministry centers should match imagery to local reading.

values.keywords: uganda yellow, east africa yellow gold, political color
values.answer: In Uganda, Yellow is the color of the NRM party that has held power 35+ years. For Ugandans, yellow can read as political before brand. East Africa ministry centers should be aware. We don't change the brand for it — we use Yellow Gold sparingly in those contexts and lean on Black + White + HCFM Blue when the political read is a risk.

values.keywords: ireland color, europe culture
values.answer: In Ireland, black is normalized everyday dress — the mourning association is present but doesn't dominate. Marian Blue carries strong devotional weight given Ireland's Catholic heritage. The full HCFM palette translates cleanly there.

values.keywords: why same brand globally, brand consistency vs culture, fragmentation
values.answer: Research from Bottomley and Doyle (2006) found that brand consistency with appropriate connotations matters more than locale-specific palette switching. If we let every market pick its own colors, we fragment back to the state we started from. The right answer is one global system applied with cultural awareness — adjusting how we use the palette, not what is in the palette.

values.keywords: wcag aa, contrast ratio explanation
values.answer: WCAG AA is the global accessibility standard. Body text needs 4.5:1 contrast. Large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold) needs 3:1. AAA is the higher tier — 7:1 for body. HCFM Blue on White is 8.6:1 (AAA). Yellow Gold on White is 1.9:1 (fails AA). White on HCFM Blue is 8.6:1 (AAA). Yellow Gold on Black is 11.7:1 (AAA).

values.keywords: why never gold body text, gold readability
values.answer: Yellow Gold body text on white is 1.9:1 contrast — far below the 4.5:1 WCAG AA minimum for body text. The text technically appears, but a quarter of readers cannot read it confidently — including older eyes, mild colorblindness, low-light viewing, and any photo-overlay context. Yellow Gold is for headlines and accents only. On dark backgrounds, it sings.

values.keywords: blue on blue, marian blue on hcfm blue
values.answer: Marian Blue on HCFM Blue scores 2.96:1 — fails WCAG AA entirely. The two blues live in the same family and lose distinction when stacked. Use White or Yellow Gold on HCFM Blue instead. Reserve Marian Blue for accents on Black or White.

values.keywords: why playlist script rules, why strict colors script
values.answer: Playlist Script's elegance comes from specific letterform thicknesses and curves that only render legibly in a narrow band of colors. Yellow Gold, White, and Marian Blue all have enough lightness contrast against dark backgrounds to preserve those curves. Other colors flatten the strokes and make the script look messy. We tested others — they don't hold up.

values.keywords: why script single words, short phrases playlist
values.answer: Script fonts lose readability quickly the longer they get. A single word in Playlist Script reads as elegant. A full sentence reads as decorative noise. Think of Playlist Script as seasoning — just the right amount elevates a dish, too much overwhelms it. Three to four words maximum, one element per design.

values.keywords: why whitney, whitney choice reason
values.answer: Whitney (Hoefler & Frere-Jones) was chosen because it is humanist sans-serif — clean and modern but warm, not cold like Helvetica or geometric like Futura. It scales from a 12px label to an 84px display headline without losing character. Six weights give us range from a quiet caption to a confident hero. Whitney does brand work that pure-geometric sans cannot do.

values.keywords: why calluna, calluna body text choice
values.answer: Calluna (Jos Buivenga) is a serif designed for long-form reading. Its serifs reflect tradition, depth, and reflection — exactly the feel a faith ministry needs for prayer text, donor letters, and reflections. Calluna is also remarkably readable at small sizes, which matters because most HCFM body text appears on phones.

values.keywords: why three fonts, why not one font
values.answer: Three fonts give three registers. Whitney is the workhorse for clarity — labels, headlines, navigation. Calluna is the body voice — reflections, letters, longer prose. Playlist Script is the warm accent for one decorative word. One font would feel monotone. Four would feel scattered. Three matches how HCFM actually communicates.

values.keywords: humanist sans, sans serif character
values.answer: Whitney is a humanist sans-serif — built on calligraphic skeletons rather than geometric ones. The lowercase 'g' has an open lower bowl, the 'y' has a curved descender, the 'V' is wider than Helvetica's. These small differences add up to a font that reads more human than Helvetica or Arial — important for a ministry where warmth matters.

values.keywords: why one or two elements, restraint design
values.answer: Combining too many design elements creates visual clutter that fights the message. A clean, focused design always outperforms a busy one. Restraint isn't about being boring — it's about every element doing one job, none competing. The four approved elements (Thin Border, Color Fade, Curved Shapes, Dark Overlays) are tools, not requirements.

values.keywords: why dark overlay opacity range, overlay 40 to 70
values.answer: Overlay opacity isn't one fixed number because images vary. A light, sparse image with one short headline only needs 40% to make text readable. A busy, photo-rich image with longer copy needs 70% or more. The rule is readability — adjust until the text passes. There is no "correct" opacity divorced from the image underneath.

values.keywords: why thin border 3 4 pixels, border thickness rule
values.answer: Borders 3-4px maximum because any thicker reads as a frame around the content rather than an accent on it. A heavy border draws the eye to the edge instead of the content. A thin border catches just enough to suggest formality without competing. Keep it 3-4px and lean Muted Gold for traditional contexts, Yellow Gold for digital.

values.keywords: why dark mode matters, dark mode percentage
values.answer: Over 80% of users browse with dark mode enabled on their phones. When you design with a black background, your design fits naturally into how the audience already views their feed. Light backgrounds in a dark feed become visual noise — dark backgrounds become continuous space.

values.keywords: why social fast, two seconds scroll, attention span
values.answer: On social, you have maybe two seconds, maybe less before someone scrolls past. That window does not allow for anything subtle. Dark background plus high-contrast Yellow Gold gives the brain its easiest possible read — figure-ground separation in milliseconds. Slow elegance happens after the scroll has stopped.

values.keywords: why ikea blue but not blue background, industry brand vs social
values.answer: IKEA and Walmart use Blue + Yellow as their brand identity — but on social media they use product photos, lifestyle images, and dark overlays. Their everyday social posts don't use blue backgrounds either. Brand identity (logo, store, packaging) is one thing. Social media application is another. The HCFM system honors both.

values.keywords: why print different, digital vs print colors
values.answer: In print, white is the canvas and brand colors step into supporting roles. Letterheads, certificates, and donor materials lean on White with HCFM Blue and Muted Gold. In digital, Black is the canvas and Yellow Gold and Marian Blue do the supporting work. The medium dictates the role. Same brand, two registers.

values.keywords: why pantone 871 muted, muted gold reason
values.answer: Muted Gold (Pantone 871C / #89764B) reads as traditional, reverent, devotional — the gold of liturgical glory and Catholic heritage. It belongs on certificates, donor acknowledgements, formal print where its quiet warmth is right. On a backlit screen it loses life; in print under reflected light it glows.

values.keywords: why whitney calluna pair, sans serif body pairing
values.answer: Whitney + Calluna works because the contrast is functional, not decorative. Whitney's sans-serif clarity for headlines tells you "this matters." Calluna's serif warmth for body text tells you "now sit and read." The pairing mirrors the cadence of how HCFM communicates — declarative headers, reflective prose.

values.keywords: why not just one font, monotype
values.answer: Using a single font for everything flattens hierarchy. Without contrast between headline and body, the reader can't tell what matters most at a glance. Three fonts give three jobs. Whitney announces. Calluna reflects. Playlist Script accents. Each font earns its place by doing what the others can't.

values.keywords: why simplified palette, 20 colors to 6
values.answer: The old palette had 20 colors (3 primary + 8 extended + 9 light). Too many. Every ministry-center designer made different choices, and the brand fragmented across regions. The 2026 system simplifies to 4 primary + 2 accent + 3 liturgical = 9 colors total, with clear rules on when each is used. Fewer choices, sharper brand.

values.keywords: why playlist script added, why script font 2026
values.answer: Playlist Script was added in 2026 to introduce warmth and humanity the system was missing. Whitney is clean and modern. Calluna is reverent and serious. Neither can do "the family that prays together stays together" with the right emotional weight. Playlist Script — used sparingly — adds the warmth a global family ministry brand needs.

values.keywords: research backing, evidence for choices, why trust this system
values.answer: Brand decisions are anchored in research: WCAG accessibility standards for contrast ratios, Labrecque & Milne (2012) on color and brand personality (Blue → competence), Madden et al. (2000) on cross-cultural color meaning, Bottomley & Doyle (2006) on brand consistency vs locale-switching, and Gestalt figure-ground principles for scroll-stop performance. The choices have a paper trail.

values.keywords: why ten beads, why not five or seven, rosary decade reason
values.answer: Ten beads because a Rosary decade has ten beads. Each Hail Mary is a bead, and ten Hail Marys form a decade — the basic unit of Marian prayer. The mark didn't pick ten arbitrarily; it picked the smallest unit of the prayer that founds HCFM. Anything fewer would be incomplete; anything more would lose the reference.

values.keywords: why circular mark, why round logo
values.answer: The circular shape is intentional. Circles communicate unity, continuity, and gathering — families coming together in prayer, the continuous rhythm of daily devotion, the unbroken cycle of the Rosary. A square or angular shape would carry different meanings. The circle is doing theological work, not just aesthetic work.

values.keywords: why each shape person, why ten people
values.answer: Each of the ten shapes represents a person in prayer — hands raised or folded. No single shape stands alone. Each depends on the others. The mark visualizes Father Peyton's vision: families linked together through prayer, forming something greater than themselves. The form is the message.

values.keywords: why central not local, why one canonical
values.answer: A single canonical brand keeps every ministry center recognizable as one family. If every center designs its own variant, the brand fragments — donors and partners can't tell which ministry is which. Centralizing the editable source means there is exactly one right version, and any change goes through one team. Predictability over flexibility.

values.keywords: why ai files restricted, why not give source files
values.answer: AI files are restricted because once a ministry has the editable source, edits start happening. Slightly different spacing, slightly different gold, slightly different wordmark — and over a year you have 24 different versions of the logo across 24 ministries. Restricting AI to brand owners (Victoria and Emmanuel) means there is one canonical logo, always.

values.keywords: why password gate, why gated downloads
values.answer: Brand assets are gated to keep them inside the HCFM creative network — ministry centers, vendors with active work, partners. Open downloads invite misuse: a vendor takes the file for an unrelated project, a third party builds something off-brand, the mark shows up where it shouldn't. The password is friction by design.

values.keywords: why two voice registers, voice why two
values.answer: HCFM speaks to two very different audiences. The faithful — families and parishes — need warmth, narrative, reverence. Vendors and partners — designers and printers — need specifics, dimensions, file formats. One voice can't serve both. Two registers acknowledge the truth that different audiences need different language, while staying recognizably HCFM in both.

values.keywords: why family the family that prays, why peyton phrase
values.answer: Father Peyton's phrase is real, original, and his. "The family that prays together stays together" came from his ministry directly. Using his actual words — instead of paraphrasing or modernizing — keeps HCFM connected to its founder. The phrase is also why the brand voice is family-first: he meant household prayer, not abstract piety.

values.keywords: why named not abstract, why specific countries
values.answer: We say "France, Philippines, Chile, Bangladesh, the United States" instead of "globally distributed" because names carry weight that abstractions lose. A reader who hears "globally" thinks "everywhere and nowhere." A reader who hears specific country names sees the mission as real and located. Specificity is mission language.

values.keywords: why real not stock, authentic photography
values.answer: Stock photography looks like stock photography — and audiences know. A staged "diverse family praying" photo from a stock library carries less weight than an actual photo of an actual ministry-center event. Real images, even when imperfect, carry credibility stock cannot match. Especially for a ministry where authenticity is half the message.

values.keywords: why photography rules, visual standards photo
values.answer: Photography rules exist because the wrong image can undo a hundred right design decisions. A grainy or off-brand photo on a perfect layout still reads as off-brand. A great photo on a mediocre layout reads as authentic. The image carries more weight than the design around it, so the bar for image quality is high.

values.keywords: why this brand page, purpose of brand portal
values.answer: This brand page exists because SharePoint blocks external consultants, vendors, and ministry centers from accessing brand materials. The team was repeatedly fielding the same brand questions over email. Ministry centers in 18 countries couldn't find a clean reference. A single canonical destination — public-facing for documents, gated for production files — solves all three problems.

values.keywords: why public, why visible to everyone
values.answer: Most of the brand page is intentionally public because the brand wants to be seen and used correctly. Ministry-center designers, vendors, and curious partners shouldn't have to email anyone to learn what HCFM Blue's hex is. Production files are gated. Reference is open. That's the right division.

values.keywords: ewtn colors, word on fire, catholic media, competitor brand
values.answer: Major Catholic media organizations all converge on the same color logic. EWTN uses Navy + Gold + White (60-25-15). Word on Fire (Bishop Barron) uses Black + Orange-Gold + White. Knights of Columbus uses Navy + Gold + White. Vatican News uses White + Gold + Deep Red. The pattern: dark or formal foundation, gold as sacred accent, restrained palette. HCFM's Black + Yellow Gold is industry-aligned with the most recognized Catholic brands.

values.keywords: vatican colors, vatican gold, catholic standard
values.answer: The Vatican standard is Gold + White + Deep Red. Gold appears in nearly every major Catholic organization's palette because it signals tradition, divinity, and continuity with Church history. HCFM's Yellow Gold is a digital-modern reading of the same gold-as-sacred convention. We're not inventing — we're translating Catholic visual heritage for a mobile-first audience.

values.keywords: why not light blue dominant, why not teal
values.answer: Healthcare-style light blue is almost never used by serious Catholic organizations as a dominant color. It reads secular, modern, clinical. Marian Blue (#00A9E0) belongs at HCFM as an accent — a 10-20% color tied to Mary specifically. Using it heavily would distance HCFM from the Catholic media tradition (EWTN, Vatican, Word on Fire) it sits inside.

values.keywords: industry alignment, why dark like everyone else
values.answer: When EWTN, Word on Fire, and Knights of Columbus all anchor their digital identity in dark backgrounds, that is not coincidence — it's convergent design. Dark backgrounds are the practical answer to small screens, dark mode, and Catholic gold-on-dark heritage. HCFM moving toward black on social aligns with industry leaders rather than departing from them.

values.keywords: why digital first, mobile audience, digital channels
values.answer: Three realities drove the 2026 update. (1) Digital-first audiences: Over 80% of HCFM engagement happens on mobile devices. Colors and typography must be optimized for screens, not just print. (2) Reaching younger generations: To fulfill Father Peyton's vision, the visual language must feel inviting to young parents and children, not dated. (3) Global consistency: 18 countries, one recognizable identity.

values.keywords: why younger generations, reach young families
values.answer: Father Peyton's mission is the family that prays together. Future families are formed today by young parents and children — and those audiences live in feeds, not in mailers. The visual system has to feel vibrant and alive to land with them. Yellow Gold says invitation. Muted Gold says formality. The mission needs invitation.

values.keywords: mobile screens optimization, screen vs print
values.answer: Muted Gold (#89764B) was designed for print materials and formal documents. On mobile screens and social feeds, it appears dark and lacks contrast. Yellow Gold (#FFB500) is optimized for digital visibility — it cuts through a crowded feed. The two golds aren't in competition; they serve different mediums.

values.keywords: why global community, family rosary audience, who follows hcfm
values.answer: The HCFM audience is a global community, not a US ministry with a few international branches. The Family Rosary Facebook page is 59.4% Filipino — only 9.4% American. India, Bangladesh, Indonesia round out the top five. The brand is built for the Manila family checking their phone before the Rosary, not just the Easton donor. This shapes every visual decision.

values.keywords: follower decline, why we updated brand
values.answer: Before the June 2025 visual update, the Family Rosary page had nine consecutive months of net follower loss. The audience was leaving. After the dark-themed update, the trend reversed. Followers came back. That isn't aesthetic preference — that's the audience telling us what works for the way they actually consume content.

values.keywords: why ab test, why test colors, why not just decide
values.answer: Color decisions used to come from preference and intuition. The 2026 system let data decide. Same content, same imagery, same fonts — only background color changes. Test across markets, measure engagement, follow the result. That's how we know the dark theme works: it doesn't guess, it shows.

values.keywords: why paid boosting, organic vs paid testing
values.answer: Organic posts are shown by Meta's algorithm to users most likely to engage — that's a curated audience, not a random sample. Paid boosting distributes the post to a broader, more representative slice, producing cleaner data and reducing the algorithm's selection bias. Even a small budget makes the comparison meaningful.

values.keywords: allan qualitative research, two kinds of research
values.answer: There are two complementary research traditions at HCFM. Allan Mirasol's qualitative work over 2+ years (focus groups, 30-300 sample sizes per country) measured emotional perception — how does this make you feel? Emmanuel's analytical work measured behavioral performance — what do people actually click, share, save? Both are valid. The brand integrates both: behavior tells us what works, emotion tells us when to be careful.

values.keywords: sub brand, podcast logo, ministry sub-brand
values.answer: Sub-brands (a podcast, an event, a sub-ministry) follow a clear rule: parent brand always wins. Use HCFM Blue and Gold palette. Use Whitney or compatible typeface. Anchor on the HCFM Circular Mark. The worst outcome is a sub-brand that creates a second competing visual identity — two different logos with two different fonts in the same room will always fight each other.

values.keywords: why simplicity logo, logo design rules
values.answer: Every serious analysis of top-performing brand logos finds the same trait: one strong visual idea, generous white space, type readable at 50×50 pixels. Your logo will most often appear as a tiny thumbnail on a phone. That's the design environment. Everything else is secondary. HCFM's mark passes that test: ten interconnected shapes that read clearly even at favicon size.

values.keywords: why no extra icons, avoid microphone trope
values.answer: For a podcast or sub-brand, don't add a microphone, headset, or sound-wave icon. Like putting a camera on a movie poster — it tells people how it's made, not what it's about. The HCFM mark is already a circle of family in prayer. That's the metaphor. Add a microphone and you're telling the listener "this is a podcast." They already know.

values.keywords: why narrative voice, why stories not bullets
values.answer: Stories carry more weight than bullet lists for the family audience. Bullet points are for vendors and processes. Pastoral letters, prayer reflections, devotional content — these need narrative motion. Walking the reader through an idea ("Notice how... Building on what we just said...") earns trust that bullets can't.

values.keywords: why warm voice, why not formal
values.answer: HCFM voice is warm, not formal — because we meet families at the kitchen table, on the bus, beside the grandmother. Formal language puts distance between the ministry and the household. Warm language closes that distance. We can be reverent and warm at the same time. Father Peyton was both.

values.keywords: why family unit, household focus
values.answer: The unit of HCFM communication is the household, not the individual. Catholic ministries often default to addressing isolated believers ("you, the seeker"). HCFM addresses the family unit because that's where Father Peyton said prayer happens. The grammar of every HCFM line treats the family as the subject of the sentence — not the brand, not the institution, the family.

values.keywords: why gradual change, why phased rollout
values.answer: Brand migrations happen gradually for a reason. When a system changes too abruptly, ministry centers don't adopt — they resist. Allan Mirasol's key insight: "If you're going to change something, do it in phases." The 2026 system honors that: ministry centers transition as they reprint, not as a forced cutover. Existing materials phase out over 6-12 months. New work uses the new system.

values.keywords: why ministry support, easton creatives support, team support
values.answer: Victoria and Emmanuel will review your design before you publish, help you apply the rules in your specific situation, create custom materials when you have a partner organization, commission logo files when the existing pack doesn't cover what you need, and run training calls for ministry-center design teams. The brand book is the floor; we are the safety net for everything above it.

values.keywords: why centralized asset distribution, sharepoint replacement
values.answer: The old SharePoint system blocked external consultants, vendors, and ministry centers from accessing brand materials. Two of the main download links on SharePoint were broken. Ministry centers in 18 countries couldn't find a clean reference. This brand portal exists to be the one place anyone — internal, external, ministry, vendor — can go for the canonical source.

values.keywords: why one accent per design, one not three
values.answer: One accent color per design because three competing accents is visual chaos. If Yellow Gold is your accent, commit. Don't add Marian Blue and Muted Gold on top. Pick one and let it lead. The 60-30-10 rule already gives you structure — adding a fourth competing color breaks the hierarchy that makes the design read clearly.

values.keywords: why dark overlay specific opacity, 40 to 60 percent overlay
values.answer: Dark overlays at 40-60% opacity hit the sweet spot. Below 40%, text on photos becomes hard to read on busy backgrounds. Above 60%, the overlay overwhelms the photo and the image stops doing its job. The exact percentage depends on the image — adjust until the text passes a quick readability test at arm's length.

values.keywords: why platform dimensions exact, why sizes matter
values.answer: Platform dimensions matter because the algorithms enforce them. Designs that fall short get cropped. Designs that overshoot get downsized awkwardly. A 1080×1080 Instagram square works. A 1000×1000 design becomes pixelated. A 1200×1200 design gets compressed. Hit the dimensions exactly — there is no benefit to "close enough."

values.keywords: why brand consistency matters, consistency mission
values.answer: Consistency is mission. When ministry centers in 18 countries use the same colors, fonts, and visual elements, families recognize HCFM instantly — the Manila family scrolling at 7 am sees the same brand the Dublin family sees at 7 pm. Recognition builds trust. Trust strengthens mission. Inconsistency does the opposite — it makes us look like 24 different organizations doing 24 different things, when we're one family doing one thing.

values.keywords: why aesthetic vs strategic, not aesthetic preference
values.answer: These are strategic improvements, not aesthetic preferences. Yellow Gold over Muted Gold for digital isn't about taste — it's about contrast on screens. Black backgrounds aren't a fashion choice — they're how 80%+ of users browse. The 60-30-10 rule isn't a designer's opinion — it's how the brain processes color hierarchy. Every choice has a reason. None of them are personal preference.

values.keywords: why this brand book, value of brand guidelines
values.answer: Without documented rules, design decisions vary widely across centers. Some use thick borders, others thin. Some use random color gradients. Some use a competing font. Documenting elements (Thin Border 3-4px, Color Fade brand-only, Dark Overlay 40-60%) replaces guesswork with guardrails. Creators stop spending energy on "is this OK?" and start spending it on the actual message.

values.keywords: what stayed the same, what didn\
values.answer: The 2026 update was refinements, not a rebrand. What stayed: HCFM Blue (#0047BB) is still the signature color. Black and White are still core. The circular mark is unchanged. Logo usage rules (no stretching, distorting, cropping) remain. Minimum sizes (1.5" print / 150px web) remain. Photography principles (faith, family, prayer, community, hope) remain. Most of what ministry centers were already doing is still right.

values.keywords: why not full rebrand, why refinement only
values.answer: A full rebrand would have meant abandoning the work ministry centers had already done — and the equity that work built. Refinement preserves what was working (the mark, HCFM Blue, the circular geometry, photography direction) while updating only what wasn't (palette overload, undefined typography, no documented design rules). You don't throw out a house to fix a kitchen.

values.keywords: google color test, why test color, data driven design
values.answer: Even sub-pixel color differences produce measurable behavioral changes at scale. Google famously tested 41 shades of blue for ad links — the winning shade generated $200M/year more revenue than the runner-up. The lesson for HCFM: small color choices compound. Yellow Gold over Muted Gold, Black over Blue background — these aren't cosmetic tweaks. They're measurable performance levers.

values.keywords: labrecque research, color and brand personality
values.answer: Labrecque & Milne's 2012 research established that color maps to brand personality dimensions. Blue significantly predicts Competence. Red significantly predicts Excitement. High saturation positively influences Excitement. For a ministry built on trust, Blue's competence association is structural — that's why HCFM Blue anchors formal materials, certificates, and donor communication.

values.keywords: cyr trust research, culturally appropriate colors
values.answer: Cyr, Head & Larios (2010) found that culturally incongruent color choices reduce trust even when they attract attention. What stops the scroll isn't always what builds the relationship. The HCFM dark + Yellow Gold system threads this carefully — high contrast for attention, then Marian Blue and warm Calluna prose for the trust that has to follow. Both registers in one system.

values.keywords: cta over vanity, click rate matters, why ctr
values.answer: Colum's framing: "What's going to lead you to take action?" Likes are habitual. Reactions are low-effort. Followers lag. The metrics that matter for a mission-driven org are shares (someone attached their identity to the content), saves (Instagram, indicates value worth returning to), and CTR to website (the design moved someone toward action). Vanity metrics tell flattering stories. Action metrics tell true ones.

values.keywords: negative feedback rate, hide post signal
values.answer: Negative Feedback Rate (hides + "not interested" clicks) is a color-avoidance signal. If one variant triggers significantly more hides than another, that's a trust or warmth failure regardless of what it's scoring on engagement. We watch for this because attention without affinity hurts mission engagement, even when the click numbers look good.
